item.title: I used to hate complex spreadsheet formulas and then I found Python in Excel
item.itunes:title: I used to hate complex spreadsheet formulas and then I found Python in Excel
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I used to pride myself on being an Excel wizard, but the reality was that I was spending more time fixing broken links and debugging #REF! errors than actually analyzing data.

That cycle ended the moment I integrated Python into my workflow. By shifting from rigid grid-based logic to flexible libraries, I turned my most tiring weekly tasks into a one-click solution.

5 Python libraries to extend Excel’s capabilities

Python libraries that supercharge Excel

6
By  Parth Shah

Why Excel formulas fail at scale

The breaking point

My breaking point with Excel didn’t come from a complex financial model; it came from something as simple as a monthly sales report. I had a master sheet that pulled data from twelve different tabs — one for each month.

To get my final numbers, I was using a combination of VLOOKUPs and SUMIFs across workbooks. I decided to insert a single new column for ‘Region’ in the January source file. Suddenly, a sea of #REF! errors flooded my dashboard. Because my formulas relied on fixed cell positions, my one tiny change had shifted the entire foundation.

I spent the next three hours manually updating cell references in hundreds of formulas. Also, my formula grew as my data grew. In a standard Excel cell, the logic is buried if I have a formula like the one below.

=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2, 'Data'!$A$1:$Z$1000, 4, FALSE), IF(B2="Direct", C2*1.1, C2))

To audit it, I have to click into the formula bar and trace colored boxes across the screen.

The Python advantage

There are many

The moment I stopped trying to chain together Excel formulas and started using the =PY() function, my productivity soared. By bringing Python’s libraries like Pandas, Matplotlib, and Seaborn directly into my workbook, I turned into a data architect.

In my previous workflow, if I wanted to look up a value, apply a discount based on multiple conditions, and handle errors, I would end up with a nested IF inside an XLOOKUP that was impossible to read.

Because Python in Excel allows for multi-line code in a single cell, I can document my thought process with comments.

Excel formulas are great at calculating, but they are bad at cleaning. If I had a column of messy names with weird spacing and inconsistent casing, I used to need three helper columns with TRIM, PROPER, and CLEAN.

With Python integrated, I just reference my table and use .str.strip().str.title(). One line of code replaces an entire chain of helper columns.

Another neat trick is when you have a giant Excel table with dozens of rows and columns and simply want to summarize it. I can use .describe() to get all the relevant information with a simple code (check the screenshot above).

My favorite part is visualization with Python. I have spent too much time fighting with Excel’s recommended charts. To get a professional-grade heap map or a complex joint plot, I usually have to jump through hoops.

By using Seaborn directly in an Excel cell, I can create publication-quality visuals that pull directly from my spreadsheet data. Just like Pivot charts, these are data-driven insights that update automatically when my table changes.

I have saved hours that used to be spent formatting axis labels and tweaking legend positions.

If you're interested in learning how to use Python in Excel, take a look at my post for detailed, step-by-step instructions.

Python libraries to explore

The learning curve

When I first typed =PY into a cell, I wasn’t just using a new function; I was unlocking a range of useful libraries. However, you don’t need to learn about everything from day one.

If you only learn one library, make it Pandas. In Excel, we are used to thinking in cells. In Pandas, you think in DataFrames (basically super-powered tables).

I use it to merge five different sheets at once, filter out irrelevant data with one line of code, and perform complex pivots that would normally take ten minutes of clicking.

Subscribe for Python-in-Excel tips, libraries, examples, and more

Turn fragile formulas into maintainable code — subscribe to the newsletter for practical Python-in-Excel walkthroughs, library-focused recipes (Pandas, Seaborn, NumPy), and clear examples that demonstrate replacing brittle formulas with readable code, and other practical workflows.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Excel’s built-in charts are fine for a quick bar graph, but they can be a nightmare to customize. I use Seaborn to create Heatmaps and Violin Plots directly in my spreadsheet cells.

When I’m dealing with massive datasets where Excel starts to slow down, I turn to NumPy. It is designed for high-performance computing. If I need to perform heavy-duty array math, NumPy does it in a fraction of the time.

Now, the transition isn’t seamless. I had to spend some time first to get there. A missing comma or a misplaced indentation will stop your work in no time.

I spent my first few days constantly Googling ‘how to filter a dataframe’ or ‘how to reference a cell in Python.’ It takes a bit of work to start thinking in df[‘ColumnName’] (and stop thinking in $A$1).

Can Python do everything Excel can?

Exploring Python’s capabilities versus Excel

3
By  Parth Shah

Stop fighting cells

Of course, Python won’t replace every Excel function out there. You need to balance between the two to unlock a smarter workflow. Also, it does require a learning curve. If you are new to Python, you can always use NotebookLM to fly through the basics.

After all, the goal isn’t just to work faster — it’s to work smarter. So, what are you waiting for? Take that first step, replace your most frustrating formula with a Python library, and see the results yourself.

item.title: Modern projectors have a bigger problem than lamp life, and nobody's talking about it
item.itunes:title: Modern projectors have a bigger problem than lamp life, and nobody's talking about it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you built a home theater a decade ago, you know the lingering anxiety that comes every time you hit the power button. There's a ticking clock inside every aging projector, a backlight bulb with a finite lifespan that's progressively eroded every time you spend an hour binging Friends for the umpteenth time. Until very recently, the very hardware that made home theater projectors so elite and expensive was the reason they failed, and it typically wouldn't happen in one swift blow. You'd see the lamp's brightness tank rapidly until the picture is unwatchable.

Today, the projector landscape has fundamentally shifted. The color wheel may have survived even on the mid-range models, but they're now eating into the sophisticated, silicon-heavy smart TV market that dominates living rooms. To facilitate this switch, backlights resembling glowing hot balls of fire have made way for quieter, well-cooled LED and laser tech. The industry wants you to believe that the shift to solid-state lighting has solved the longevity problem, but that's the least of your concerns with Android TV OS and Google TV also in the literal picture now. The hardware isn't your primary enemy anymore, but the software sure is.

This projector gives me a 120-inch 4K screen for gaming that's cheaper than most high-end monitors

Gaming in 4K60 for that superior immersion.

By  Chandraveer Mathur

When the lamp was the weakest link

A little context for why lamp half-life is impertinent now

To understand the modern projector dilemma, we have to look at the baseline of the legacy hardware we left behind. With conventionally lit projectors reliant on Ultra High-Performance (UHP) or Metal Halide lamps, service life was a major and constant concern. These traditional light sources were rated anywhere from 2,000 to 5,000 hours, but that was literally just the half of it. Long before the bulb actually popped, it would reach a half-life point where its brightness degraded to around 50% of the original output. By the time a 3,000-hour rated UHP lamp hit 1,500 hours, it was often way too dim for normal use, particularly if you had any ambient light in your room.

Moreover, lamp life was the weakest link that defined when you throw a projector out, simply because older models from 15 years ago typically shipped with absolutely no software skin. You got firmware for a basic OSD, and if you dropped serious cash on a high-end model, that firmware might control a mechanized lens for optical zoom, basic automatic keystone, or motorized focus. The firmware was a closed static loop that merely drove the display, and didn't try serving double duty as an input source.

To fix the half-life problem, the projector industry at large pivoted to LED and Laser light engines. If you look at spec sheets today, the numbers are staggering. BenQ notes that their LED products boast a life expectancy of 50,000 hours. Soundcore claims their LED bulbs can last anywhere from 20,000 to 50,000 hours. Xgimi goes even further, stating that some LED lamps can give you up to 60,000 hours of use. These are all half-life numbers, totaling a whopping 30 years of operation before backlight dimming to unusable levels, assuming four hours of daily use. Even doubled to eight hours a day, 17 years is thoroughly respectable and a far cry from the 5,000-hour halide bulbs.

The switch comes with several advantages, like much better cooling, because LEDs and their driver circuits aren't 700°F orbs of glass. Consequently, their cooling fans run quieter, making integrated speaker designs viable again. I also love how LEDs achieve peak brightness from the get-go, unlike metal halides which took a hot minute (intentional pun). More expensive models even use dedicated RGB LEDs to eliminate the color wheel and related artifacting. However, a bigger problem is afoot.

The smart TV Trojan horse

And a parched oasis of updates

Regardless of the lighting tech, the modern projector is gunning to replace smart TVs in every home. LED lamps are making ridiculously tiny form factors and models at multiple price points viable for hardware OEMs. What worries me is all the market leaders shipping their projectors with Android TV OS or Google TV, a MediaTek SoC, a few gigabytes of RAM, and onboard eMMC storage. It connects to Wi-Fi, decodes AV1 streams, and processes Google Assistant voice queries just like a smartphone would, but with a 120-inch display.

Sure, the LED or laser light source still has a finite lifespan measured in hours, but I worry consumers don't realize the metric that determines the death of your projector hardware has shifted from hours of illumination to years of software support. When you buy a Google Pixel or a Samsung Galaxy, the brands clearly promise five years of major Android OS updates and seven years of security patches. You know exactly when your device will reach End of Life (EOL) and can budget for an upgrade accordingly. In glaring contrast, projector OEMs offer no such transparency. Most brands do not commit to a specific number of years for software support, nor do they guarantee any Android version updates.

The Android version itself might seem minor in the grand scheme of how a projector is used — after all, as long as Netflix and Plex open, who cares if you are on Android 11 or Android 14? However, the underlying Google Play Services and Widevine DRM certifications dictate whether those apps will continue to function. Seeing companies like Xgimi publicly commit to five years of software updates for their premium Horizon and Titan lines is rare and commendable, but even then, the core Android version that ships remains largely unchanged. You will receive OTA (Over-The-Air) patches that squash bugs, optimize image processing, and update the launcher, but that doesn't include a platform upgrade.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter projector buys

Don't buy another projector blind—subscribe to the newsletter for focused analysis on projector longevity, software risks, and buying trade-offs. Get clear comparisons and expert takeaways that help you choose hardware that lasts for years.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

To me, this reeks of planned obsolescence. With Google pushing app devs to support the latest API levels to target the larger Android user base on smartphones, it's a matter of time before your projector throws in the towel or an indie app dev drops support for the version your projector's running because it's too much effort to maintain. If that doesn't kill it, in five years apps become heavier, memory management becomes a bottleneck, and the SoC chokes up even when the LED lamp is ready to go another 25 years.

Projectors are now a dangerous hardware-software duet, just like everything else

In the worst case, you could try rooting and sideloading an Android build on your projector, but it risks bricking the proprietary firmware-level routines for ToF sensors, CMOS cameras, and co-processors that perform screen adaptation, automatic keystone correction, obstacle avoidance, screen alignment, and mechanized autofocus. That's when the realization finally sets in that software is the new Achilles' heel for the average home projector.

Brands continue marketing stellar and worry-free lamp life with new LED tech as a fix-all for buyers still evaluating their purchase the old way. But if you're in the market today, take a step back and rethink how much you'd want to spend on something that'll only last as long as its onboard software.

To understand the modern projector dilemma, we have to look at the baseline of the legacy hardware we left behind. With conventionally lit projectors reliant on Ultra High-Performance (UHP) or Metal Halide lamps, service life was a major and constant concern. These traditional light sources were rated anywhere from 2,000 to 5,000 hours, but that was literally just the half of it. Long before the bulb actually popped, it would reach a half-life point where its brightness degraded to around 50% of the original output. By the time a 3,000-hour rated UHP lamp hit 1,500 hours, it was often way too dim for normal use, particularly if you had any ambient light in your room.

Moreover, lamp life was the weakest link that defined when you throw a projector out, simply because older models from 15 years ago typically shipped with absolutely no software skin. You got firmware for a basic OSD, and if you dropped serious cash on a high-end model, that firmware might control a mechanized lens for optical zoom, basic automatic keystone, or motorized focus. The firmware was a closed static loop that merely drove the display, and didn't try serving double duty as an input source.

To fix the half-life problem, the projector industry at large pivoted to LED and Laser light engines. If you look at spec sheets today, the numbers are staggering. BenQ notes that their LED products boast a life expectancy of 50,000 hours. Soundcore claims their LED bulbs can last anywhere from 20,000 to 50,000 hours. Xgimi goes even further, stating that some LED lamps can give you up to 60,000 hours of use. These are all half-life numbers, totaling a whopping 30 years of operation before backlight dimming to unusable levels, assuming four hours of daily use. Even doubled to eight hours a day, 17 years is thoroughly respectable and a far cry from the 5,000-hour halide bulbs.

The switch comes with several advantages, like much better cooling, because LEDs and their driver circuits aren't 700°F orbs of glass. Consequently, their cooling fans run quieter, making integrated speaker designs viable again. I also love how LEDs achieve peak brightness from the get-go, unlike metal halides which took a hot minute (intentional pun). More expensive models even use dedicated RGB LEDs to eliminate the color wheel and related artifacting. However, a bigger problem is afoot.

The smart TV Trojan horse

And a parched oasis of updates

Regardless of the lighting tech, the modern projector is gunning to replace smart TVs in every home. LED lamps are making ridiculously tiny form factors and models at multiple price points viable for hardware OEMs. What worries me is all the market leaders shipping their projectors with Android TV OS or Google TV, a MediaTek SoC, a few gigabytes of RAM, and onboard eMMC storage. It connects to Wi-Fi, decodes AV1 streams, and processes Google Assistant voice queries just like a smartphone would, but with a 120-inch display.

Sure, the LED or laser light source still has a finite lifespan measured in hours, but I worry consumers don't realize the metric that determines the death of your projector hardware has shifted from hours of illumination to years of software support. When you buy a Google Pixel or a Samsung Galaxy, the brands clearly promise five years of major Android OS updates and seven years of security patches. You know exactly when your device will reach End of Life (EOL) and can budget for an upgrade accordingly. In glaring contrast, projector OEMs offer no such transparency. Most brands do not commit to a specific number of years for software support, nor do they guarantee any Android version updates.

The Android version itself might seem minor in the grand scheme of how a projector is used — after all, as long as Netflix and Plex open, who cares if you are on Android 11 or Android 14? However, the underlying Google Play Services and Widevine DRM certifications dictate whether those apps will continue to function. Seeing companies like Xgimi publicly commit to five years of software updates for their premium Horizon and Titan lines is rare and commendable, but even then, the core Android version that ships remains largely unchanged. You will receive OTA (Over-The-Air) patches that squash bugs, optimize image processing, and update the launcher, but that doesn't include a platform upgrade.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter projector buys

Don't buy another projector blind—subscribe to the newsletter for focused analysis on projector longevity, software risks, and buying trade-offs. Get clear comparisons and expert takeaways that help you choose hardware that lasts for years.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

To me, this reeks of planned obsolescence. With Google pushing app devs to support the latest API levels to target the larger Android user base on smartphones, it's a matter of time before your projector throws in the towel or an indie app dev drops support for the version your projector's running because it's too much effort to maintain. If that doesn't kill it, in five years apps become heavier, memory management becomes a bottleneck, and the SoC chokes up even when the LED lamp is ready to go another 25 years.

Projectors are now a dangerous hardware-software duet, just like everything else

In the worst case, you could try rooting and sideloading an Android build on your projector, but it risks bricking the proprietary firmware-level routines for ToF sensors, CMOS cameras, and co-processors that perform screen adaptation, automatic keystone correction, obstacle avoidance, screen alignment, and mechanized autofocus. That's when the realization finally sets in that software is the new Achilles' heel for the average home projector.

Brands continue marketing stellar and worry-free lamp life with new LED tech as a fix-all for buyers still evaluating their purchase the old way. But if you're in the market today, take a step back and rethink how much you'd want to spend on something that'll only last as long as its onboard software.

item.title: Please, for the love of all things holy, update your BIOS
item.itunes:title: Please, for the love of all things holy, update your BIOS
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I don't care if you call this a PSA, an explainer, or even a hot take. If you leave this article and decide it's time to update your BIOS, I've done my job. Despite how useful new BIOS releases can be for your system overall, there's still a reluctance to update unless something has gone seriously wrong with your system. That's outdated advice, and it's high time to address it.

The default BIOS/UEFI settings aren't great: Here's what to set instead

PCs, laptops, and motherboards often ship with BIOS/UEFI settings that leave power on the table. Let's fix that.

6
By  Joe Rice-Jones

A BIOS update should be your first troubleshooting step, always

Don't trust what you reach in the patch notes

I'm not under any illusions that a BIOS update is part of your regular PC update process. There are plenty of new BIOS releases that are irrelevant to most users, especially in an era with multi-year chipset and socket support, so updating to every new BIOS release just because accomplishes little more than wasting time. The moment something is off with your PC, though, a BIOS update should be the first thing you reach for. That's true even if you assume the update won't fix your problem.

Here's the issue. The release notes that accompany new BIOS updates are terrible, and it's easy to fall into the trap of assuming an update won't fix your issue if you're not locked in on all the latest tech news (and I do mean all of it). Take the example above from the Asus Prime B860 Plus. This is a massive update that can not only fix issues, but also improve performance. It'd be hard to know that from the release notes, though. Here's what's going on:

  • Intel microcode 0x114 supposedly improves performance on Arrow Lake desktop CPUs.
  • MRC adjusts timings on boot, potentially solving RAM-related issues.
  • Intel's Management Engine firmware (ME FW) can improve performance and fix security vulnerabilities.
  • GOP, or Graphics Output Protocol, gives you display output in your UEFI BIOS and can solve GPU-related issues.
  • RST VMD, or Intel's Rapid Storage Technology driver, can solve SSD-related issues.

That's not even everything included in this one BIOS release, and it can potentially solve issues related to your memory, storage, and GPU, as well as offer a performance boost to your CPU. These are release notes from Asus, too, which generally provide more information than other brands.

You'll normally see three things in a BIOS, in order from most to least common: improved memory compatibility, microcode or AGESA, and support for new CPUs. If you're having an issue with your PC, it's easy to write off a BIOS update that simply promises improved memory compatibility if your memory isn't acting up. Still, it's important to remember that everything in your system plugs into your motherboard. If you leave your BIOS unaddressed during troubleshooting, you're working with a broken foundation no matter what the release notes say.

Modern BIOS updates can enable new features

Your BIOS is a critical component of your PC

You didn't need to think much about your BIOS in years past. It was just a way to initialize all of your hardware, and if you were really willing to get your hands dirty, you could pop in to change a few settings that otherwise wouldn't be available in your operating system. That's not the case anymore. Both AMD and Intel lean on robust BIOSes from their partners to enable features like AMD's Precision Boost Overdrive (PBO), and they increasingly rely on motherboard brands to optimize performance after launch.

First, features. Occasionally, new BIOS updates will add new features that can improve your performance. A recent example of this came from AMD, when board partners updated their BIOSes with the X3D Turbo Mode. This mode is specifically for 3D V-Cache CPUs like the Ryzen 9 9950X3D, which can offer up to an 18% performance improvement in games. That's huge, and it's a feature that arrived through a BIOS update.

6 BIOS settings every new PC builder needs to know about

We know you want to install your games, but first, you need to handle a few things in the BIOS

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones

New features are few and far between, but you'll commonly find microcode updates with new BIOS releases, which can be significant. Intel calls microcode by its name, while AMD calls it AGESA, but they're essentially the same thing. Microcode is like a BIOS for your CPU. It wakes the cores, sets the voltages, and gives AMD and Intel a way to issue updates to their hardware without recalling sold chips.

New microcode updates can improve performance. The advantages are small from update to update, but over the course of a chipset's lifecycle, microcode can make a solid difference. In recent years, it's also been used to improve performance after launch. Intel released microcode to improve the performance of Arrow Lake CPUs, as mentioned above, and AMD released a new AGESA to improve the lackluster performance of Ryzen 9000 CPUs when they were first released.

In addition, AMD and Intel have used microcode to solve big issues. The biggest example of this was Intel with its Core i9-13900K and Core i9-14900K, which experienced dire instability issues due to improper voltages. That was addressed with a microcode update. Last generation, a slew of AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D CPUs were burning themselves up in the CPU socket, again due to improper voltages. AMD addressed the issue with a new AGESA in a matter of days.

I upgraded to the AMD Ryzen 7 9800X3D from the Intel Core i9-14900K, and it was the best upgrade I ever made

It wasn't just the technical problems of Intel that pushed me away, either.

30
By  Adam Conway

You don't need to be aware of what each microcode update changes, or even what's going on in each BIOS release. The vast majority of the time, upgrading your BIOS will either do nothing, or give you a little extra performance and solve issues before they become catastrophic. It's extremely rare for a new BIOS to hurt your PC or lower your performance; and in those cases, a quick Google search will turn up dozens of articles telling you if a recent BIOS is problematic.

BIOS updates aren't what they used to be

Old habits die hard

If BIOS updates are so great, why don't we do them more commonly? I suspect there are a couple of reasons. First, it's just inconvenient. You have to find a flash drive, format it, reboot into your BIOS, and hang around for upwards of 15 to 20 minutes for the flash to finish. Even if you know how to update your BIOS, and you've done it before, going through the process is annoying.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter BIOS & PC tips

Stop guessing—subscribing to the newsletter gives clear, actionable BIOS and firmware guidance plus hands-on troubleshooting and practical PC hardware tips so you can update confidently, avoid common pitfalls, and keep systems stable.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The bigger reason I suspect there's reluctance is that updating your BIOS can brick your motherboard, but it's very important these days not to conflate that with the idea that a BIOS update will brick your motherboard. That's just not true in the vast majority of cases. Years ago, when BIOS updates did little more than solve unforeseen platform issues, the general wisdom was to only update your BIOS if you have a specific reason to. Today, that's not the case.

A ton of motherboards available today come with a dual BIOS feature. That's true even down to mainstream chipsets. You're able to store your BIOS on the board itself and flash a new one, and should you lose power during the flashing process, you can revert to the original BIOS and try again. Even without a dual BIOS feature, nearly every modern motherboard has safeguards in place to recover from a power outage during a BIOS update. And if you're really, really worried, you can always plug your PC into a UPS so it's safe from a power outage while flashing a new BIOS.

These 2 BIOS features make bricked PCs a thing of the past

The old days of worrying during every BIOS update are gone

7
By  Joe Rice-Jones

A BIOS update can solve a world of hurt

BIOS updates aren't something you should be afraid of. They're a core pillar of enabling your PC to reach its peak performance, and I've encountered dozens of PCs where a simple BIOS update solved a multitude of issues. From overloaded RAM to GPUs not being recognized to boot failures to poor game performance, a BIOS update fixed these issues. Those are all real situations I've encountered, too.

Regardless of what gets you on board, make sure to keep your BIOS update to date. Your PC will thank you.

item.title: This lightweight Linux distro is what old hardware has been waiting for
item.itunes:title: This lightweight Linux distro is what old hardware has been waiting for
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There’s a certain kind of older PC that never really gets a dignified ending. It still powers on, the keyboard still feels fine, and the screen still has some life left in it, but modern operating systems start treating it like dead weight. Every update adds a little more drag. Every background service feels like one more thing the machine didn’t ask for. At some point, the hardware itself isn’t the problem. It’s the software piled on top of it.

Old computers usually don’t need anything flashy. They just need an operating system with a little discipline.

That’s why Void Linux feels like such a surprise when you try it on aging hardware. It doesn’t have the name recognition of Ubuntu, Fedora, or Linux Mint, and honestly, that’s part of the appeal. Void has its own package manager, uses runit instead of systemd, and offers both glibc and musl variants, all of which help make a distro that feels leaner than many mainstream options. On an older laptop or desktop, that difference doesn’t stay abstract for very long. You feel it almost immediately when the machine starts acting useful again.

4 reasons Q4OS is the best operating system to breathe some new life into your old laptop

Q4OS is an underrated distro for owners of dinosaur PCs and laptops

6
By  Ayush Pande

Why Void Linux makes old computers feel quick again

Less background baggage gives old hardware a fighting chance

One of the first things you notice with Void Linux is how little it seems interested in doing behind your back. That sounds simple, but it matters a lot when you’re dealing with hardware that has little headroom left. Older CPUs and small amounts of RAM don’t have the patience for bloated defaults, extra daemons, or desktop features nobody asked for. Void doesn’t show up, trying to impress you with layers of hidden activity. It mostly just gets out of the way.

That restraint makes a bigger difference than spec sheets usually admit. Apps open with less hesitation, boot times stay reasonable, and the whole machine feels less like it’s negotiating every basic task. You’re not constantly paying a performance tax for features designed around newer hardware. That’s the line between an old laptop that feels annoying and one that still feels legitimately handy. When you’re trying to keep aging hardware alive, that line matters a lot.

Void’s design helps explain why it lands this way. Its in-house XBPS package manager is quick, and runit keeps the system feeling smaller and cleaner than many heavier alternatives. You can feel that philosophy in everyday use, especially on hardware that no longer has the brute force to mask wasted motion. Old computers usually don’t need anything flashy. They just need an operating system with a little discipline.

Void Linux works best when simplicity is the whole point

A leaner distro can stretch useful life much farther

Void Linux also feels like a smarter fit for older computers because it doesn’t pretend every install should be built the same way. That matters when you’re trying to squeeze more life out of a ten-year-old laptop, an old office desktop, or a secondhand mini PC. A lot of mainstream distros still claim modest requirements, but their default experience keeps drifting upward over time. Void feels more comfortable with the idea that a machine can exist to browse the web, write, play local media, and handle a few dependable apps without trying to be everything at once. That narrower target is a strength.

Its package ecosystem fits that same mindset. XBPS is fast, the repositories are tidy, and the option for separate glibc and musl branches gives more experienced users real control over how they want to build things. You’re not just installing something lightweight and calling it a day. You’re stepping into a distro that still seems to believe Linux can be modular, flexible, and a little opinionated without becoming obnoxious. On older hardware, that can make the whole machine feel less like a relic and more like something worth keeping around.

There’s also something refreshing about a distro that hasn’t sanded off every sharp edge in pursuit of mass appeal. Void isn’t trying to be the easiest recommendation for everybody, and that gives it a more distinct identity than a lot of bigger names have these days. Once you’re settled in, it feels stable, focused, and surprisingly calm. It wins by being fast and deliberate, not by throwing glossy convenience at every problem. On older hardware, that kind of focus can feel like a luxury.

The same strengths can also make Void harder to recommend

Some people need easy defaults more than clever efficiency

That said, Void Linux is not a magic spell for every aging computer. A lot of people rescue older hardware because they want something easy, not because they’re looking for a side project. They want an old laptop that can handle email, web browsing, documents, and maybe some streaming without becoming a full-time hobby. In that situation, a distro with broader community support, more beginner tutorials, and friendlier defaults can still be the better answer. Void can be elegant and efficient, but that doesn’t automatically make it welcoming.

Its differences from mainstream Linux distros can also create real friction. Void uses runit instead of systemd, and XBPS instead of APT or DNF, which means a lot of familiar Linux advice doesn’t translate cleanly. That’s not a flaw by itself, but it does raise the skill floor. If someone is already unsure about Linux, introducing unfamiliar tools and workflows might not feel empowering. It might just feel like they picked the weird path by accident.

There are practical limits to the whole “just install a lighter distro” argument, too. Yes, Void supports several architectures and can absolutely help older, lower-power devices feel less burdened, but the hardware’s age is only one part of the problem. Sometimes the real issue is a dying battery, flaky Wi-Fi, a failing hard drive, or a screen that’s barely hanging on. No distro can rescue hardware that’s physically falling apart. At best, it can stop decent hardware from being written off too early.

Even so, Void Linux is still a compelling choice

Efficiency matters more than mass-market hand-holding here

Even with those caveats, I still think Void Linux earns the “secret weapon” label for old hardware. The main reason is that it respects the machine you’re running on. It doesn’t assume endless resources, and it doesn’t pile on extra complexity in the name of convenience. On aging hardware, that attitude goes a long way. Plenty of machines get called obsolete when what they really need is an operating system with lower overhead and fewer assumptions.

Yes, there’s a learning curve, but that’s also part of the appeal. Void asks you to pay attention, and in return, it gives you a system that feels easier to understand once you’ve settled into it. Services don’t feel buried under layers of abstraction. Package management feels quick and direct. Even the choice between glibc and musl reinforces that this is a distro for people who care about how their systems are built, not just how polished the defaults look. That makes it a stronger long-term fit for old hardware than a beginner distro that grows heavier every release.

Void Linux’s minimum specs are almost ridiculously low

Void Linux doesn’t ask for much, at least if you’re talking about the base install rather than a heavier desktop setup. That makes it a great little proof point for the article, because it shows just how little hardware the distro actually needs to get moving. Void’s handbook lists these as the recommended minimums for most installations, while also noting that Xfce images need more resources.

Edition / architecture

Minimum CPU

Minimum RAM

Minimum storage

x86_64-glibc

x86_64

520MB

700MB

x86_64-musl

x86_64

520MB

600MB

i686-glibc

Pentium 4 with SSE2

520MB

700MB

Those specs won’t make every ancient PC feel modern, especially once you add a full desktop and heavier apps, but they do explain why Void Linux feels so comfortable on hardware other distros have already left behind. It helps that Void leaves room for practical compromises. You can keep the base system lean while still using broader app distribution methods when needed. That means running Void doesn’t have to feel like signing up for a niche lifestyle. It can just be a smart way to keep an older PC fast and useful. Old hardware doesn’t need dogma. It needs a setup that stays light without making daily use feel like homework.

Why more old PC owners should pay attention to Void

Old hardware doesn’t always need to be replaced, and it definitely doesn’t need to be treated like e-waste just because mainstream software keeps getting heavier. Void Linux makes a strong case that the right operating system can uncover a lot of usefulness that was still there the whole time. It feels lean without feeling stripped down. It feels intentional without feeling like a stunt. That combination is a lot rarer than it should be.

Plenty of machines get called obsolete when what they really need is an operating system with lower overhead and fewer assumptions.

Void won’t be the right choice for everyone, and that’s perfectly fine. What it offers is more interesting than broad appeal anyway. It offers a version of Linux that still feels sharp, efficient, and a little bold, which is exactly what a lot of old hardware needs. For the right user, that’s more than a good fit. It’s the kind of distro that makes an aging computer feel worth using again.

Void Linux

Void Linux is a lightweight, independent distro built for speed, simplicity, and control. Its lean design makes it a great fit for older hardware and minimal setups. It’s not the most beginner-friendly option, but it can make aging PCs feel surprisingly useful again.
item.description:
There’s a certain kind of older PC that never really gets a dignified ending. It still powers on, the keyboard still feels fine, and the screen still has some life left in it, but modern operating systems start treating it like dead weight. Every update adds a little more drag. Every background service feels like one more thing the machine didn’t ask for. At some point, the hardware itself isn’t the problem. It’s the software piled on top of it.

Old computers usually don’t need anything flashy. They just need an operating system with a little discipline.

That’s why Void Linux feels like such a surprise when you try it on aging hardware. It doesn’t have the name recognition of Ubuntu, Fedora, or Linux Mint, and honestly, that’s part of the appeal. Void has its own package manager, uses runit instead of systemd, and offers both glibc and musl variants, all of which help make a distro that feels leaner than many mainstream options. On an older laptop or desktop, that difference doesn’t stay abstract for very long. You feel it almost immediately when the machine starts acting useful again.

4 reasons Q4OS is the best operating system to breathe some new life into your old laptop

Q4OS is an underrated distro for owners of dinosaur PCs and laptops

6
By  Ayush Pande

item.title: I replaced Portainer, Grafana, and Prometheus with this stack
item.itunes:title: I replaced Portainer, Grafana, and Prometheus with this stack
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
After spending a few weeks running self-hosted tools individually on a puny Raspberry Pi Zero, I made the shift to Docker. Containerizing self-hosted tools has numerous advantages over running them individually because one hiccup results in a total system halt. Docker’s container isolation makes it easy to manage, and Portainer was the very first thing I used. My first impression was good because I finally got a visual tool to manage my Docker containers and didn’t need to spin up compose files or run long commands in the terminal.

Prometheus and Grafana were the next additions because Portainer didn’t offer me any sort of hardware monitoring. After spending a good amount of time with these tools, I’ve finally made my shift to Dockhand, Beszel, and Glance. This trio fulfills all of my container management, monitoring, and dashboard needs, and fills huge gaps existing in the previous stack.

Picking Dockhand over Portainer

I crave simplicity

Portainer isn’t a bad tool by any means. I’ve used it extensively for almost all of my Docker journey because I want to keep terminal interaction to a minimum. As a Windows user, I’ve always been a GUI-inclined person, and when Portainer can offer that for a mere 100 MB resource usage, I’m all up for it.

The biggest problem for me with Portainer is the UI design. It tries to squeeze in everything in the app, and I don’t need those features. The main dashboard lacks details and only shows the connected environment with a few other details. Dockhand shows more information in a presentable manner that’s easy on my eyes.

If you use Portainer, there’s a high chance that you spend most of your time on the Containers page. I don’t know how Portanier missed it, but if you want to take an action on a container, you must first select it and then use the top bar to do an action like start, stop, restart, etc. Dockhand shows quick actions next to each container, which is convenient. I can modify the visual appearance to make the whole interface easier on the eyes.

Even the stack creation and management experience is much better. I don’t need to scroll through a huge page to type the compose file content and then add environment variables. Everything is available in a single-page format. Another good thing about Dockhand is that it makes container updates child’s play. I can simply check for updates from the container management page, and it highlights all those that have a new image.

Portainer doesn’t have any kind of update button for such tasks, and the only option is to either recreate each container, which pulls the newest image from DockerHub. Or you can set up Watchtower to manage updates, but that project is no longer in active maintenance, and you have to use forks or find another tool.

9 Docker containers that run 24/7 on my $100 mini PC

Maximum value budget homelab.

15
By  Samir Makwana

Ditching Grafana and Prometheus

Beszel is better

I’m always amazed at how Beszel managed to put out such a visually nice tool with such a small resource footprint. I used Grafana and Prometheus in my early days, but that was a horrible experience. Grafana is an absolute no for a beginner and requires you to put time into configuring what resources you want to monitor. Even if you manage to do that, the tool is more suitable for a serious, enterprise environment rather than a small home lab with a few servers.

I can also not ignore Grafana’s heavy resource usage and how it impacts my low-end servers. Beszel, on the other hand, doesn’t even consume 50 MB when it's active and shows me rich, pre-configured graphs after deployment. Beszel keeps the complexity aside and only requires you to set up an agent on every machine.

Monitoring multiple Docker systems is possible in Beszel, and I can take a peek at my Raspberry Pi and old laptop’s current usage with the web UI. Beszel’s focused on simplicity and also has an alert system baked into it. It can trigger alerts on the dashboard whenever a certain hardware use exceeds the programmed limit. Even if you don’t want to use the alerts, the tool is sufficient for occasional checks and the container’s status.

Glance adds a dashboard

No more tab struggles

Glance replaces my need to open, pin, and bookmark several tabs. It helps me build a single-page dashboard that can display the status of my Docker systems, active containers, and combine it with other information such as RSS feeds, YouTube and Twitch activity, weather, and more. It’s my default home page of the browser because it shows me a lot of information on a single page and lets me decide which course to take.

Subscribe: Better Docker self-hosting tips in the newsletter

Get concise, practical deep dives by subscribing to the newsletter—tool comparisons, step-by-step configuration ideas, monitoring strategies, and dashboard tips, including self-hosted Docker setups, so you can build lean, manageable home-lab stacks.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

I don’t open Dockhand unless I need to edit or modify the containers. Since Glance can show whether the container or service is active or not, I can take instant action from the dashboard. Clicking on a container item redirects me to its web UI, and I don’t need to add bookmarks or open Dockhand for such a trivial purpose.

Glance supports granular customization to add or remove elements using the YML file. I can add more elements and build a multiple-page dashboard, but I’m content with the current view.

Lean, feature-rich, and less confusing

Letting go of Grafana, Prometheus, and Portainer makes my setup lean and doesn’t inflict load on my system resources. I don’t have to think twice before deploying my new trio on any device, be it an SBC, mini PC, or a desktop. The trio is visually appealing, packs features I need for container management, updates, monitoring, and access, and suits a small-scale home labber like me.

Containerizing self-hosted tools has numerous advantages over running them individually because one hiccup results in a total system halt. Docker’s container isolation makes it easy to manage, and Portainer was the very first thing I used. My first impression was good because I finally got a visual tool to manage my Docker containers and didn’t need to spin up compose files or run long commands in the terminal.

Prometheus and Grafana were the next additions because Portainer didn’t offer me any sort of hardware monitoring. After spending a good amount of time with these tools, I’ve finally made my shift to Dockhand, Beszel, and Glance. This trio fulfills all of my container management, monitoring, and dashboard needs, and fills huge gaps existing in the previous stack.

Picking Dockhand over Portainer

I crave simplicity

Portainer isn’t a bad tool by any means. I’ve used it extensively for almost all of my Docker journey because I want to keep terminal interaction to a minimum. As a Windows user, I’ve always been a GUI-inclined person, and when Portainer can offer that for a mere 100 MB resource usage, I’m all up for it.

The biggest problem for me with Portainer is the UI design. It tries to squeeze in everything in the app, and I don’t need those features. The main dashboard lacks details and only shows the connected environment with a few other details. Dockhand shows more information in a presentable manner that’s easy on my eyes.

If you use Portainer, there’s a high chance that you spend most of your time on the Containers page. I don’t know how Portanier missed it, but if you want to take an action on a container, you must first select it and then use the top bar to do an action like start, stop, restart, etc. Dockhand shows quick actions next to each container, which is convenient. I can modify the visual appearance to make the whole interface easier on the eyes.

Even the stack creation and management experience is much better. I don’t need to scroll through a huge page to type the compose file content and then add environment variables. Everything is available in a single-page format. Another good thing about Dockhand is that it makes container updates child’s play. I can simply check for updates from the container management page, and it highlights all those that have a new image.

Portainer doesn’t have any kind of update button for such tasks, and the only option is to either recreate each container, which pulls the newest image from DockerHub. Or you can set up Watchtower to manage updates, but that project is no longer in active maintenance, and you have to use forks or find another tool.

9 Docker containers that run 24/7 on my $100 mini PC

Maximum value budget homelab.

15
By  Samir Makwana

Ditching Grafana and Prometheus

Beszel is better

I’m always amazed at how Beszel managed to put out such a visually nice tool with such a small resource footprint. I used Grafana and Prometheus in my early days, but that was a horrible experience. Grafana is an absolute no for a beginner and requires you to put time into configuring what resources you want to monitor. Even if you manage to do that, the tool is more suitable for a serious, enterprise environment rather than a small home lab with a few servers.

I can also not ignore Grafana’s heavy resource usage and how it impacts my low-end servers. Beszel, on the other hand, doesn’t even consume 50 MB when it's active and shows me rich, pre-configured graphs after deployment. Beszel keeps the complexity aside and only requires you to set up an agent on every machine.

Monitoring multiple Docker systems is possible in Beszel, and I can take a peek at my Raspberry Pi and old laptop’s current usage with the web UI. Beszel’s focused on simplicity and also has an alert system baked into it. It can trigger alerts on the dashboard whenever a certain hardware use exceeds the programmed limit. Even if you don’t want to use the alerts, the tool is sufficient for occasional checks and the container’s status.

Glance adds a dashboard

No more tab struggles

Glance replaces my need to open, pin, and bookmark several tabs. It helps me build a single-page dashboard that can display the status of my Docker systems, active containers, and combine it with other information such as RSS feeds, YouTube and Twitch activity, weather, and more. It’s my default home page of the browser because it shows me a lot of information on a single page and lets me decide which course to take.

Subscribe: Better Docker self-hosting tips in the newsletter

Get concise, practical deep dives by subscribing to the newsletter—tool comparisons, step-by-step configuration ideas, monitoring strategies, and dashboard tips, including self-hosted Docker setups, so you can build lean, manageable home-lab stacks.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

I don’t open Dockhand unless I need to edit or modify the containers. Since Glance can show whether the container or service is active or not, I can take instant action from the dashboard. Clicking on a container item redirects me to its web UI, and I don’t need to add bookmarks or open Dockhand for such a trivial purpose.

Glance supports granular customization to add or remove elements using the YML file. I can add more elements and build a multiple-page dashboard, but I’m content with the current view.

Lean, feature-rich, and less confusing

Letting go of Grafana, Prometheus, and Portainer makes my setup lean and doesn’t inflict load on my system resources. I don’t have to think twice before deploying my new trio on any device, be it an SBC, mini PC, or a desktop. The trio is visually appealing, packs features I need for container management, updates, monitoring, and access, and suits a small-scale home labber like me.

item.title: Your home network is only as fast as its slowest cable, and most people have at least one bad one
item.itunes:title: Your home network is only as fast as its slowest cable, and most people have at least one bad one
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Home networking can feel particularly frustrating in the fact that it's easy to feel like you've done everything right, but still end up with a network that feels off. You have that well-reviewed router, respectable internet plan, and you've even gone through the effort of running Ethernet to the devices that need it most. And yet, speeds are inconsistent, file transfers slow to a sloth-like pace, and there are so many moments that just feel off.

Most people in this situation start looking at their router settings, or call their ISP to complain, or start researching a Wi-Fi 7 upgrade they probably do not need, and while it's certainly possible the problem lies within one of those avenues, a single underperforming cable in the wrong place can poison the well just as fast as any other misconfiguration.

5 reasons you might want to upgrade to 2.5GbE for your home network

With more devices supporting 2.5GbE, upgrading your home network is worth thinking about

3
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Your network is only as fast as the slowest link

A fundamental truth of networking

To understand why a single cable matters so much, it helps to think about how data actually moves through a home network. Traffic doesn't just teleport from one device to the next, but instead, it travels through a physical chain: from your modem, to your router, then out to any switches or access points, then finally to your devices.

Every cable involved in that chain has a ceiling, or a maximum speed that it's capable of. If you were to connect your router straight to your computer with no devices in-between, that cable only affects your computer if it were to be under-specced. But if you have an underperforming cable sitting at a more central point in your network, say, from your router to a switch, that cable ends up acting as a bottleneck.

It does not matter how capable the hardware on either side of a cable is. If the cable connecting them is the weak point, the hardware's potential goes unrealized. Your router can be capable of multi-gigabit throughput, and a single degraded cable between it and your modem will ensure you never actually see those high speeds in practice.

I wired my house with Cat6 and regret not spending the extra $50

Get cabling right the first time.

39
By  Rich Edmonds

Not all cables are created equal

Nor do they last forever

When you think of a "bad" Ethernet cable, most people would think of one that has been poorly terminated, chewed through, or has some obvious physical defect. In reality, the cables that are most likely to be hurting your network will look completely fine from the outside.

Ethernet cables are rated for different speeds depending on their category.

A Category 5e cable, or Cat5e, is rated for 1Gbps at up to 100 meters, which was perfectly adequate for a long time, and are found in many homes. But multi-gigabit internet plans are no longer exotic, and 2.5Gbps router ports have become increasingly common in the mid-range. If you are pushing those kinds of speeds through a Cat5e cable, there is significant performance left on the table. Cat6 supports 10Gbps at shorter distances and handles higher frequencies more cleanly, while Cat6a extends that 10Gbps rating to 100 meters. The cable category running between your most important network nodes genuinely matters when the hardware on either end is capable of more than Cat5e can deliver. Many homes will have Cat5e in the walls, as that was the most economical choice at the time the homes were built or renovated. If you're using your RJ45 jacks to connect your devices and expecting 2.5 GbE speeds but not seeing them, there's a good chance the problem lies within the cable runs in your walls.

Then there is age and physical condition, which can't be understated. Ethernet cables do degrade. The copper inside oxidizes over time, especially at connector contacts, and cables that have been bent sharply, zip-tied aggressively, or run under furniture legs can absolutely develop internal damage that is invisible from the outside.

2.5GbE is the real home upgrade, not 10GbE vanity bandwidth

Unless market conditions are right for the higher speed, of course

13
By  Joe Rice-Jones

A lot of users won't saturate a Cat5e connection

If your devices don't support anything above 1Gbps, it doesn't matter

If your home internet plan tops out at 300 or 500 Mbps, an aging Cat5e cable running between your modem and router is not your problem. The theoretical ceiling of that cable is still well above what your ISP is delivering, and in that scenario, spending money on cable upgrades is not going to move the needle on your internet speeds.

Wi-Fi has also improved to the point where a well-placed router can comfortably outperform what many internet plans can deliver from the wall, so for mainstream use cases, the cable conversation is almost moot.

3 myths about RJ45 Ethernet cables that I get tired of hearing

We're dispelling the most commonly heard and pervasive myths about Ethernet cables that just won't die.

29
By  Andre Revilla

Raw internet speed isn't the full story

Home network traffic carries more than just internet

While it is true that most internet plans won't saturate a Cat5e link, local network traffic absolutely can, and if you're planning on running any kind of home server or home lab, understanding the limits of your cables is crucial.

Consider a NAS drive sitting on your network. Backing up a large photo library or transferring a video project between machines is entirely local traffic, and it will push a cable far harder than your internet connection ever will. A degraded cable between your router and a network switch can introduce packet errors that cause retransmissions, which show up as transfers that are slower than they should be, or ones that stall altogether. Also, if you have two 2.5 GbE capable devices, but there's a Cat5e link somewhere in the chain, the entire transfer gets slowed down by more than half of the expected bandwidth.

Wi-Fi 7 routers are finally here, but most homes aren't wired well enough to take advantage

Your home is probably not ready for Wi-Fi 7, and won't be for a long time

14
By  Tanveer Singh

The fix is relatively cheap, if you think it's worth it in the first place

The good news is that Ethernet cables are inexpensive and replacing them is one of the lowest-effort upgrades you can make to a home network. A quality Cat6 cable long enough to run between most home network nodes costs a few bucks. If you are not sure where to start, focus on the cables connecting your most central hardware first: modem to router, router to any switches, and any cables that are visibly aged or have lived a hard life tangled behind a desk.

item.title: Affinity is a great Adobe alternative, but these underrated open-source apps are even better
item.itunes:title: Affinity is a great Adobe alternative, but these underrated open-source apps are even better
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Affinity going free after being acquired by Canva was a major deal in the design community. People started canceling their Adobe subscriptions because we now have the old Affinity Photo, Designer, and Publisher in one unified free app - Affinity. For most people on Windows and Mac, it basically settled the endless “Adobe alternatives” hunt. However, Linux users were still left out, and there’s also the looming fear that Canva could paywall features, plus, some users might not want to create an account just to create basic graphics.

None of this makes Affinity bad. I personally think it’s the best free tool for design out there right now, but it does make it inaccessible or unattractive to some. And that’s where open-source fills the gap. I’ve covered plenty of open-source graphics apps, but not all of them are made equal or even available on Linux like you’d expect. So here are my top open-source recommendations that are cross–platform and actually handle the same things that Affinity does.

Affinity is free now, so why even bother with open-source?

It’s about accessibility and control

The most practical route for most people would simply be to cancel their Adobe subscriptions and move over to Affinity. But that’s not an option for Linux users, and it’s not particularly appealing if you don’t love the idea of needing a Canva account just to open your design software . Or trusting that “free forever” will stay that way especially when a company with investors is footing the bill.

Open-source doesn’t have that problem. Nobody can quietly move features behind a paywall, and the software runs whether the company behind it lives or dies. You also don’t typically need to worry about creating accounts or what data is being collected. And of course, the biggest win is that a lot of open-source tools are usually built by and for Linux users.

So for anyone who cares about transparency and accessibility, open-source is still the better move over Affinity. The question is just, are there actually tools that can hold a candle? I think I’ve found a few…

I replaced Adobe, Figma, and Canva with one free tool, and I'm not paying for design software again

It's an all-kill design tool

2
By  Nolen Jonker

Pinta for basic graphics

A no-nonsense option for quick edits

Pinta is usually my first recommendation for anyone looking for an open-source alternative to any of the big players, whether Photoshop, Affinity, or even GIMP. I’d say it falls somewhere between Affinity and Canva in terms of usability because of the intuitive interface.

Pinta is an open-source, cross-platform digital painting and image editing app for raster work, and it was actually specifically developed as a Paint alternative for Mac and Linux users. It’s built for all the straightforward graphics tasks like adding text and cropping, but it’s also capable of some more advanced creative edits. You get layer-based non-destructive editing, selection tools, precise property adjustments, blend modes, color control, gradients, brushes, and filters and effects.

It effectively replaces the Pixel workspace in Affinity, which used to be Affinity Photo, for graphic designs and illustrations, photo touch-ups, simple composites, annotating screenshots, or adding text and shapes to images.

Pinta
See at Pinta

Graphite for vector edits

It does what Affinity does

You might wonder why I’m recommending this over Inkscape, which is a very mature and reliable vector editor that’s available on Linux. I’m recommending Graphite for its similarities to Affinity. For starters, the interface is more modern than Inkscape and quite similar to Affinity, so you’re getting the same vibe. But more importantly, Graphite is a hybrid vector-raster editor, same as Affinity.

Graphite is a browser-based progressive web app that can be accessed from any platform, and it’s in alpha development at this time, but already very impressive. You can edit raster and vector graphics on the same canvas, but its raster-specific tools are still a bit limited right now. The vector toolkit is everything you’d expect, however, and it can functionally replace Affinity’s Vector workspace. You get all the same node controls, pen tools, text, colors, gradients, and much more.

Another cool thing about Graphite is that it has layers, as well as a node graph. Editing with nodes gives you a non-destructive procedural workflow with more control over your elements and their properties.

Graphite
See at Graphite

Scribus for print work

It’s not pretty, but it gets the job done

Publishing and layout software is not really something I reach for often since I don’t work much with print, but I do still use Affinity’s Layout workspace for digital presentations. It’s the perfect InDesign alternative, unless you’re on Linux or want open-source. In that case, you can’t go wrong with Scribus.

Scribus is an open-source cross-platform desktop publishing tool for laying out your documents for print and publication. It’s quite different from Affinity in look and features, but this is all you need if you’re working on graphics for a newsletter, magazine, brochure, book, pitch deck, and so on.

It’s a genuinely capable tool. You get master pages, CMYK color support, ICC color profiles, proper typography controls, linked text frames, and PDF export with press-ready output. It supports OpenType fonts, has a built-in script editor, and handles multi-page documents really well.

Scribus
See at Scribus

You’ve got options

When Affinity became free, I was ecstatic. It finally meant no more endless digging for the perfect free Photoshop alternative, because it was here. But the open-source crowd was never going to be satisfied with a free Canva-owned tool, and I honestly can’t blame them. I actually still have the same fear that one day we’ll open Affinity and all the good stuff will be locked behind a Pro subscription. And then there’s Linux users, for whom the free Affinity basically means nothing.

Thankfully, the open-source ecosystem doesn’t wait for Canva’s permission. And if you're dead set on trying Affinity on Linux, there's a community project that's worth checking out.
item.description:
Affinity going free after being acquired by Canva was a major deal in the design community. People started canceling their Adobe subscriptions because we now have the old Affinity Photo, Designer, and Publisher in one unified free app - Affinity. For most people on Windows and Mac, it basically settled the endless “Adobe alternatives” hunt. However, Linux users were still left out, and there’s also the looming fear that Canva could paywall features, plus, some users might not want to create an account just to create basic graphics.

None of this makes Affinity bad. I personally think it’s the best free tool for design out there right now, but it does make it inaccessible or unattractive to some. And that’s where open-source fills the gap. I’ve covered plenty of open-source graphics apps, but not all of them are made equal or even available on Linux like you’d expect. So here are my top open-source recommendations that are cross–platform and actually handle the same things that Affinity does.

Affinity is free now, so why even bother with open-source?

It’s about accessibility and control

The most practical route for most people would simply be to cancel their Adobe subscriptions and move over to Affinity. But that’s not an option for Linux users, and it’s not particularly appealing if you don’t love the idea of needing a Canva account just to open your design software . Or trusting that “free forever” will stay that way especially when a company with investors is footing the bill.

Open-source doesn’t have that problem. Nobody can quietly move features behind a paywall, and the software runs whether the company behind it lives or dies. You also don’t typically need to worry about creating accounts or what data is being collected. And of course, the biggest win is that a lot of open-source tools are usually built by and for Linux users.

So for anyone who cares about transparency and accessibility, open-source is still the better move over Affinity. The question is just, are there actually tools that can hold a candle? I think I’ve found a few…

I replaced Adobe, Figma, and Canva with one free tool, and I'm not paying for design software again

It's an all-kill design tool

2

But more importantly, Graphite is a hybrid vector-raster editor, same as Affinity.

Graphite is a browser-based progressive web app that can be accessed from any platform, and it’s in alpha development at this time, but already very impressive. You can edit raster and vector graphics on the same canvas, but its raster-specific tools are still a bit limited right now. The vector toolkit is everything you’d expect, however, and it can functionally replace Affinity’s Vector workspace. You get all the same node controls, pen tools, text, colors, gradients, and much more.

Another cool thing about Graphite is that it has layers, as well as a node graph. Editing with nodes gives you a non-destructive procedural workflow with more control over your elements and their properties.

Graphite
See at Graphite

Scribus for print work

It’s not pretty, but it gets the job done

Publishing and layout software is not really something I reach for often since I don’t work much with print, but I do still use Affinity’s Layout workspace for digital presentations. It’s the perfect InDesign alternative, unless you’re on Linux or want open-source. In that case, you can’t go wrong with Scribus.

Scribus is an open-source cross-platform desktop publishing tool for laying out your documents for print and publication. It’s quite different from Affinity in look and features, but this is all you need if you’re working on graphics for a newsletter, magazine, brochure, book, pitch deck, and so on.

It’s a genuinely capable tool. You get master pages, CMYK color support, ICC color profiles, proper typography controls, linked text frames, and PDF export with press-ready output. It supports OpenType fonts, has a built-in script editor, and handles multi-page documents really well.

Scribus
See at Scribus

You’ve got options

When Affinity became free, I was ecstatic. It finally meant no more endless digging for the perfect free Photoshop alternative, because it was here. But the open-source crowd was never going to be satisfied with a free Canva-owned tool, and I honestly can’t blame them. I actually still have the same fear that one day we’ll open Affinity and all the good stuff will be locked behind a Pro subscription. And then there’s Linux users, for whom the free Affinity basically means nothing.

Thankfully, the open-source ecosystem doesn’t wait for Canva’s permission. And if you're dead set on trying Affinity on Linux, there's a community project that's worth checking out.

item.title: A 3D printing enclosure changed my print quality more than any nozzle, filament, or slicer setting ever did
item.itunes:title: A 3D printing enclosure changed my print quality more than any nozzle, filament, or slicer setting ever did
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For a long time, I treated print quality like a puzzle made up of little upgrades. I swapped nozzles, tried better filament, tuned retraction, adjusted temperatures, and spent far too much time staring at slicer previews. Some of those changes helped, at least a bit. None of them changed the overall consistency of my prints as I had hoped.

It’s much easier to tune a printer when the room stops sabotaging it.

Then I added an enclosure, and the difference was immediate in a way most 3D printing upgrades never are. It didn't magically fix every problem, but it stabilized far more of the process than I expected. Suddenly, prints that used to feel a little unpredictable started finishing with fewer surprises. That changed how I think about quality, because it turns out the environment around the printer matters just as much as the settings on the screen.

The best 3D printing upgrade I made was designing my STLs instead of depending on community downloads

There's no substitute for ingenuity and limitless personalization

1
By  Chandraveer Mathur

An enclosure fixed the part of printing I couldn't tune

The printer needed a stable room to work

One of the most frustrating things about desktop 3D printing is how easy it is to chase the wrong variable. You can spend hours dialing in a profile and still end up with corners lifting, layers looking uneven, or tall prints developing subtle artifacts halfway through. In many cases, the printer isn’t suddenly bad, and the filament isn't suddenly junk. The problem is that the machine is printing in a room that keeps changing around it.

An enclosure cuts down on those environmental swings in a way no slicer setting ever can. Drafts from an HVAC vent, a ceiling fan, an open door, or even somebody walking past can affect how plastic cools. That matters more than many people realize, especially with materials that hate inconsistent temperatures. When the air around the print is better controlled, the whole process becomes less chaotic.

That stability showed up in my prints as fewer weird failures that didn't seem to have a clear explanation. Warping dropped, layer adhesion improved, and large, flat parts stopped acting as if they were personally offended by the build plate. Even when a print wasn't perfect, the flaws became easier to diagnose because the enclosure removed one giant source of randomness. It's much easier to tune a printer when the room stops sabotaging it.

The biggest gain was consistency across every print

Good prints became normal instead of occasional lucky wins

What really sold me on an enclosure wasn't one dramatic before-and-after test. It was the fact that good results stopped feeling accidental. Before, I could get a nice print one day and then run the same file again later with noticeably worse results. That kind of inconsistency is what makes 3D printing feel more like babysitting than building.

Once the printer was enclosed, repeatability improved more than any single hardware tweak had ever managed. The first layer became more dependable, especially on longer jobs where early instability tends to snowball. Mid-print temperature fluctuations seemed less likely to throw off the final result. I wasn't constantly wondering whether this print would be one of the good ones or one of the annoying ones.

That reliability also changed how I used the printer in practical terms. I became much more willing to take on longer jobs, print larger parts, and use materials I'd previously treated with suspicion. Instead of planning around the printer's mood, I could plan around the project. That's a much bigger quality-of-life improvement than getting slightly cleaner seams from a fancy nozzle or squeezing out one more polished profile in the slicer.

Plenty of people can print well without one

Not every setup demands another big box

To be fair, an enclosure isn't a universal requirement for good printing. Plenty of people run open-frame printers and get excellent results, especially with PLA in a room that stays fairly stable. If your printer lives in a quiet space without constant drafts or temperature swings, you may not see the same dramatic jump. In that situation, better calibration and good filament can absolutely take you pretty far.

There's also the issue of cost, space, and convenience. Enclosures take up space, and some are awkward, flimsy, or just plain ugly. Depending on the printer, maintenance can also be a little more annoying. If you're printing casually and already happy with your results, adding one more thing around the machine might feel like solving a problem you don't actually have.

It's also true that some upgrades are easier to justify because they feel more direct. A new nozzle promises cleaner detail or faster printing. A premium filament claims better tolerances. A slicer tweak gives you instant feedback and scratches that tuning itch every 3D printing enthusiast eventually develops. By comparison, an enclosure can sound boring, even slightly overhyped, because it doesn't look like a performance part.

But controlling the environment still changes everything important

Print quality improved because the process stopped drifting

The reason I still rate an enclosure so highly is simple: it improves the conditions on which every other upgrade depends. A better nozzle still has to push plastic into open air. A premium spool still has to cool predictably after it leaves the hot end. The smartest slicer settings in the world still assume the printer is operating under conditions close to stability. When that foundation is shaky, every improvement on top of it has less room to shine.

Subscribe for practical 3D-printing enclosure advice

Join the newsletter for hands-on guidance about controlling 3D‑printer environments and practical setup tips: how to choose or build enclosures, solve temperature and draft problems, and apply strategies that boost print consistency and reliability.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

That's why an enclosure felt more meaningful than the usual upgrade path. It didn't just target one symptom or one narrow weakness. It made the whole system behave more consistently from the first layer to the last. Even prints that had previously been possible became less stressful because success no longer depended on whether the room happened to cooperate that day.

There's also a mindset shift that comes with it. Once I saw how much print quality improved by controlling the environment, I stopped assuming every flaw needed a more exotic solution. Sometimes the answer isn't a new profile, a new part, or a new roll of filament. Sometimes the answer is making the printer's surroundings less chaotic, which is a lot less glamorous but a lot more effective.

A printer designed from the start to be enclosed usually works better than an open-frame model tucked into an aftermarket box because the whole machine was built around heat management, airflow, and component placement. The electronics, motion system, cooling, and even the frame layout are typically arranged to handle higher chamber temperatures without creating new reliability problems. That often means better material support, fewer compromises, and less trial and error in balancing part cooling against chamber warmth. You can absolutely improve an open printer with an enclosure, but a purpose-built enclosed machine usually feels more cohesive because enclosure performance isn't being added after the fact, it's part of the printer's design.

Why this one upgrade changed how I judge print quality

I still think nozzles, filament, and slicer settings matter, because of course they do. They just don't sit at the top of my list anymore. An enclosure made a bigger difference because it improved the printer's ability to do the basics well, over and over again, without depending on luck. That's the kind of upgrade that changes results and your relationship with the machine.

For me, that's what made the difference feel so outsized. I wasn't suddenly printing with some magical secret sauce. I just gave the printer a more predictable place to work, and that solved more real-world quality problems than any other change I'd made before it. Once that clicked, it became hard to see an enclosure as optional tuning gear rather than one of the smartest upgrades you can make.

Bambu Lab P1S
$439 $699 Save $260
Build Volume
256x256x256mm
Printing Speed
500mm/s
Materials Used
PLA, PETG, TPU, PVA, PET, ABS, ASA
Brand
Bambu Lab

If you choose the Bambu P1S, you don't need to worry about finding a good enclosure because the printer itself is already enclosed.

P1S (Without AMS)
