The printer needed a stable room to work

One of the most frustrating things about desktop 3D printing is how easy it is to chase the wrong variable. You can spend hours dialing in a profile and still end up with corners lifting, layers looking uneven, or tall prints developing subtle artifacts halfway through. In many cases, the printer isn’t suddenly bad, and the filament isn't suddenly junk. The problem is that the machine is printing in a room that keeps changing around it.

An enclosure cuts down on those environmental swings in a way no slicer setting ever can. Drafts from an HVAC vent, a ceiling fan, an open door, or even somebody walking past can affect how plastic cools. That matters more than many people realize, especially with materials that hate inconsistent temperatures. When the air around the print is better controlled, the whole process becomes less chaotic.

That stability showed up in my prints as fewer weird failures that didn't seem to have a clear explanation. Warping dropped, layer adhesion improved, and large, flat parts stopped acting as if they were personally offended by the build plate. Even when a print wasn't perfect, the flaws became easier to diagnose because the enclosure removed one giant source of randomness. It's much easier to tune a printer when the room stops sabotaging it.

The biggest gain was consistency across every print

Good prints became normal instead of occasional lucky wins

What really sold me on an enclosure wasn't one dramatic before-and-after test. It was the fact that good results stopped feeling accidental. Before, I could get a nice print one day and then run the same file again later with noticeably worse results. That kind of inconsistency is what makes 3D printing feel more like babysitting than building.

Once the printer was enclosed, repeatability improved more than any single hardware tweak had ever managed. The first layer became more dependable, especially on longer jobs where early instability tends to snowball. Mid-print temperature fluctuations seemed less likely to throw off the final result. I wasn't constantly wondering whether this print would be one of the good ones or one of the annoying ones.

That reliability also changed how I used the printer in practical terms. I became much more willing to take on longer jobs, print larger parts, and use materials I'd previously treated with suspicion. Instead of planning around the printer's mood, I could plan around the project. That's a much bigger quality-of-life improvement than getting slightly cleaner seams from a fancy nozzle or squeezing out one more polished profile in the slicer.

Plenty of people can print well without one

Not every setup demands another big box

To be fair, an enclosure isn't a universal requirement for good printing. Plenty of people run open-frame printers and get excellent results, especially with PLA in a room that stays fairly stable. If your printer lives in a quiet space without constant drafts or temperature swings, you may not see the same dramatic jump. In that situation, better calibration and good filament can absolutely take you pretty far.

There's also the issue of cost, space, and convenience. Enclosures take up space, and some are awkward, flimsy, or just plain ugly. Depending on the printer, maintenance can also be a little more annoying. If you're printing casually and already happy with your results, adding one more thing around the machine might feel like solving a problem you don't actually have.

It's also true that some upgrades are easier to justify because they feel more direct. A new nozzle promises cleaner detail or faster printing. A premium filament claims better tolerances. A slicer tweak gives you instant feedback and scratches that tuning itch every 3D printing enthusiast eventually develops. By comparison, an enclosure can sound boring, even slightly overhyped, because it doesn't look like a performance part.

But controlling the environment still changes everything important

Print quality improved because the process stopped drifting

The reason I still rate an enclosure so highly is simple: it improves the conditions on which every other upgrade depends. A better nozzle still has to push plastic into open air. A premium spool still has to cool predictably after it leaves the hot end. The smartest slicer settings in the world still assume the printer is operating under conditions close to stability. When that foundation is shaky, every improvement on top of it has less room to shine.

Subscribe for practical 3D-printing enclosure advice

Join the newsletter for hands-on guidance about controlling 3D‑printer environments and practical setup tips: how to choose or build enclosures, solve temperature and draft problems, and apply strategies that boost print consistency and reliability.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

That's why an enclosure felt more meaningful than the usual upgrade path. It didn't just target one symptom or one narrow weakness. It made the whole system behave more consistently from the first layer to the last. Even prints that had previously been possible became less stressful because success no longer depended on whether the room happened to cooperate that day.

There's also a mindset shift that comes with it. Once I saw how much print quality improved by controlling the environment, I stopped assuming every flaw needed a more exotic solution. Sometimes the answer isn't a new profile, a new part, or a new roll of filament. Sometimes the answer is making the printer's surroundings less chaotic, which is a lot less glamorous but a lot more effective.

A printer designed from the start to be enclosed usually works better than an open-frame model tucked into an aftermarket box because the whole machine was built around heat management, airflow, and component placement. The electronics, motion system, cooling, and even the frame layout are typically arranged to handle higher chamber temperatures without creating new reliability problems. That often means better material support, fewer compromises, and less trial and error in balancing part cooling against chamber warmth. You can absolutely improve an open printer with an enclosure, but a purpose-built enclosed machine usually feels more cohesive because enclosure performance isn't being added after the fact, it's part of the printer's design.

Why this one upgrade changed how I judge print quality

I still think nozzles, filament, and slicer settings matter, because of course they do. They just don't sit at the top of my list anymore. An enclosure made a bigger difference because it improved the printer's ability to do the basics well, over and over again, without depending on luck. That's the kind of upgrade that changes results and your relationship with the machine.

For me, that's what made the difference feel so outsized. I wasn't suddenly printing with some magical secret sauce. I just gave the printer a more predictable place to work, and that solved more real-world quality problems than any other change I'd made before it. Once that clicked, it became hard to see an enclosure as optional tuning gear rather than one of the smartest upgrades you can make.

Bambu Lab P1S
$439 $699 Save $260
Build Volume
256x256x256mm
Printing Speed
500mm/s
Materials Used
PLA, PETG, TPU, PVA, PET, ABS, ASA

If you choose the Bambu P1S, you don't need to worry about finding a good enclosure because the printer itself is already enclosed.

item.title: Claude subscribers just lost access to OpenClaw and other third-party tools—unless they pay more
item.itunes:title: Claude subscribers just lost access to OpenClaw and other third-party tools—unless they pay more
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Claude subscriptions no longer cover third-party tools (like OpenClaw) starting April 4th at 12pm PT.
  • To use Claude on OpenClaw, you'll need extra usage bundles or a Claude API key.
  • Anthropic blames surging demand, but subscribers get a one-time credit equal to one month.

As AI has begun to take off, we're seeing the rise of services that allow you to attach your favorite AI model to it. Such is the case of OpenClaw, which allows you to use your API of choice to create a digital assistant that can help you perform tasks that a regular LLM can only dream of doing.

For a while, using OpenClaw and Claude was a dream combination, using the utility of the former with the smarts of the latter to help people get stuff done. Unfortunately, Anthropic has just announced that if you want to continue using Claude on third-party services like OpenClaw, you're gonna have to pay more.

Claude can now automate your entire desktop, but with a serious limitation

It's likely a temporary one, though.

7
By  Simon Batt

Using Claude on OpenClaw is getting more expensive

But Anthropic is giving everyone some credit

In a post on X, Boris Cherny, head of Claude Code at Anthropic, announced the change in plans. Once 12 pm PT arrives on April 4th, 2026, anyone with a Claude subscription won't have their OpenClaw usage covered by their plan. You'll need to either purchase an extra usage bundle or use a Claude API key.

Cherny says the change is because Claude has been seeing a ton of demand lately (as is evident by the multiple times it went down in March), and Anthropic hadn't properly built third-party tool usage into the Claude subscription plans. Fortunately, Anthropic is offering up to a 30% discount on pre-purchased extra usage bundles to make the transition less painful. Plus, if you are a subscriber, Anthropic will give you a one-time credit to your account, equal to how much you've been paying every month. You'll need to redeem it by April 17th, so don't waste time grabbing it.

Cherny also mentions in the replies that using OpenClaw on a Claude subscription plan wasn't allowed in the first place, as per the Terms of Service. As such, Cherny feels this is less restricting people based on what they're using Claude for and more enforcing the rules they had set up already.

I set up Claude Code the way its creator does, and the difference is night and day

Who better to learn from than the person who made it?

18
By  Mahnoor Faisal

item.title: I set up a home server for the first time and these 5 apps changed everything
item.itunes:title: I set up a home server for the first time and these 5 apps changed everything
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Self-hosting journeys have various ways of getting off of the ground. In my case, my old gaming PC was begging for some sort of home, and instead of being relegated to storage, I decided to install Proxmox on it and give it a new lease on life. Mind you, this created another solution looking for a problem. I now had a box running in my closet drawing power, and I wasn't entirely sure what to do with it. But once I found the right software, things clicked fast, and these 5 apps made everything worth it.

It took some work, but I'm in love with these 6 self-hosted services now

Self-hosting has been an amazing journey

11
By  João Carrasqueira

Jellyfin

A functional media server

Jellyfin is a free, open-source media server that lets you stream your own library of movies, TV shows, and music to any device on your network. Point it at a folder, let it scrape the metadata on its own, and your collection of media actually has a front-end that feels good to use.

The setup was surprisingly painless. I pulled the Docker container, mapped my media directory, and had a working instance inside fifteen minutes. The first time I opened the web interface everything was laid out nicely, just as you'd expect on a subscription-based streaming service.

Day to day, Jellyfin handles everything I used to split across multiple paid subscriptions. The Android and Apple TV clients work well enough, and the browser experience is solid, and my Intel CPU has Quick Sync, making any kind of transcoding work way lighter than it would be in software.

These 6 self-hosted tools became my daily drivers after I ditched the cloud

My data, my rules

10
By  João Carrasqueira

Nextcloud

The Google Drive replacement I needed

Jellyfin was only the beginning. Nextcloud was the next app, and it made a huge difference in my productivity. It's a self-hosted file sync and productivity platform which attempts to replace Google Drive, Calendar and Contacts.

Nextcloud was the most complex app on this list to set up properly. There are a lot of moving parts with the web server, the database, the caching layer, and the cron jobs. Getting it dialed in took an evening rather than fifteen minutes, but once it was running, the payoff was immediate. I set up auto-upload on my phone for photos, synced my documents folder across two laptops, and started using the built-in calendar to replace Google Calendar entirely.

What keeps me coming back is the ecosystem. Nextcloud has an app store with add-ons for notes, bookmarks, tasks, password management, and more. It's not going to match Google's polish feature for feature, but it doesn't really need to; it's a totally adequate self-hosted replacement that does exactly what it needs to.

I use these 7 self-hosted apps instead of Google Drive, Notion, and more

Get those cloud-based services off my lawn

7
By  Ayush Pande

Immich

The perfect self-hosted photo library

Immich is a self-hosted photo and video management platform that looks and feels remarkably close to Google Photos. Automatic mobile backup, a timeline view, facial recognition, location-based browsing, smart search, essentially everything you need is there, running entirely on your own hardware.

On my system, everything was handled almost entirely through Docker Compose, and I had everything up and running within about 20 minutes. Once the overwhelming task of syncing my entire photo library up was finished and I set up my family with the mobile app, it really felt like a 1:1 replacement.

Join the newsletter for practical home server app guides

Subscribe to the newsletter for hands-on self-hosting coverage: app recommendations, step-by-step setup tips, performance trade-offs, and practical configuration notes for Jellyfin, Nextcloud, Immich, Tailscale and other tools.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Immich is a bit more resource-hungry than the other apps on this list, though. The machine learning features benefit from at least 4 GB of RAM, and a GPU helps if you have a large library. Importing an existing library from Google Takeout takes some patience; the official CLI tool works, but a third-party tool called immich-go can handle bulk imports and Google's messy metadata files a bit more gracefully. In my case, switching from my iCloud library was a bit of a hack, but once all the legwork was finished, it felt worth it.

9 Docker containers that run 24/7 on my $100 mini PC

Maximum value budget homelab.

15
By  Samir Makwana

Tailscale

Accessing everything remotely was a must

These services are great at home, but I want my family and I to be able to access them remotely in a secure way, and Tailscale allows for just that. Tailscale is a mesh VPN built on top of WireGuard, you install the client on your server and on whatever devices you want to connect, sign in, and they can see each other as if they're on the same local network. It's essentially a must if you want to access self-hosted services remotely, but it's especially important in my case, as port forwarding is a non-starter. My ISP has put me behind CGNAT, which makes accessing anything from outside my network a bit more complicated. Traditional VPN setups wouldn't work in this scenario, and Tailscale is a lifesaver.

6 free tools every home lab needs

Level up your tinkering game with these neat utilities

7
By  Ayush Pande

A home server is nothing without the apps

Once the foundation of your home server is in place, the cost of trying out new services drops to nearly zero. Spinning up another Docker container takes minutes, and since getting these five apps running at the start, I've started experimenting with dozens of others, and it has costed me nothing. If you've been on the fence about setting up a home server, the barrier to entry is lower than it looks. An old PC or a cheap mini PC, a Linux install, and Docker are really all you need to get started.
